papers.title: A hybrid machine learning framework with two-step feature selection for identifying key biomarkers and drug targets in monkeypox.
papers.abstract: Monkeypox (Mpox) is a viral disease that has garnered global attention due to its human-to-human transmissibility and cross-species transmission. Recent outbreaks in both endemic and non-endemic regions highlight the urgent need to elucidate its molecular mechanisms and evolutionary dynamics. As of now, there are no approved antiviral therapies available for the treatment of Mpox. In this work, we proposed a robust machine learning (ML) and bioinformatics approach to uncover candidate biomarkers associated with Mpox. Two microarray datasets and one RNA-seq dataset were analyzed to screen an in
papers.summary: This study uses a hybrid machine learning framework with two-step feature selection to identify key biomarkers and potential drug targets for monkeypox. By analyzing multiple gene expression datasets, the authors propose candidate genes that could be targeted for therapy, addressing the current lack of approved antiviral treatments.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis by combining computational analysis of transcriptomic data with experimental confirmation. The findings suggest that these genes could improve diagnosis and understanding of this chronic pain syndrome.

papers.title: Mining and experimental validation of machine learning-based immune-related diagnostic biomarkers for hepatocellular carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a highly malignant and aggressive tumor. Immune-related genes (IRGs) expression correlates closely with the HCC immune microenvironment, and this study aims to identify immune-related diagnostic markers in HCC.
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to identify immune-related genes that could serve as diagnostic biomarkers for hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), and validates these findings experimentally. The identified biomarkers may improve early diagnosis and understanding of the immune microenvironment in HCC.

papers.title: Development and external validation of machine learning models to predict insulin resistance among iron-deficient children and adolescents.
papers.abstract: Insulin resistance (IR) represents a critical metabolic complication in iron-deficient children, yet existing predictive models target general or overweight pediatric populations and do not account for iron-deficiency as a distinct risk modifier. This study aimed to develop and externally validate machine learning (ML) models using routinely available clinical parameters to address this diagnostic gap.
papers.summary: This study developed and validated machine learning models to predict insulin resistance specifically in iron-deficient children and adolescents, filling a gap since existing models target general or overweight populations. The models use routine clinical parameters and were externally validated, offering a practical tool for early detection in this high-risk group.

papers.title: Deep learning model based on MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for noninvasive assessment of lymphovascular invasion in rectal cancer patients.
papers.abstract: Accurate preoperative assessment of lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in patients with rectal cancer (RC) is important for guiding postoperative management. This study aimed to develop and validate a deep learning model based on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)-derived microvascular network simulation parameters for the preoperative assessment of LVI in RC patients.
papers.summary: This study developed a deep learning model using MRI-derived microvascular network simulation parameters to noninvasively assess lymphovascular invasion (LVI) in rectal cancer patients before surgery. The model showed promise for guiding postoperative management by accurately predicting LVI status.

papers.title: DeepMech: a machine learning framework for chemical reaction mechanism prediction.
papers.abstract: The ability to predict the complete, step-by-step mechanism of chemical reactions from first principles remains a grand challenge in science. The importance of chemical reaction mechanisms (CRMs) pervades almost all domains such as prebiotic chemistry, drug discovery, materials science and so on. Uncovering CRMs remains a complex task, traditionally reliant on expert-driven experiments or expensive quantum chemical computations. While deep learning (DL) studies have shown promise in predicting reaction outcomes, they have largely ignored important intermediates and mechanistic steps en route t
papers.summary: DeepMech is a new machine learning framework that can predict the complete step-by-step mechanism of chemical reactions, including intermediates and mechanistic steps that previous deep learning approaches have ignored. This addresses a major challenge in chemistry by offering a faster alternative to traditional expert-driven experiments or expensive quantum chemical computations, with potential applications in drug discovery and materials science.

papers.title: Incorporating Surfaced-Induced Dissociation Mass Spectrometry Data into an AlphaFold-derived deep learning network improves protein structure prediction.
papers.abstract: Surface-Induced Dissociation native Mass Spectrometry (SID-nMS) is a tandem MS activation method that yields information on the connectivity and stoichiometry of protein complexes. While insufficient for direct structure elucidation, the data derived from SID-nMS has considerable potential to inform multimeric protein structure prediction. We hypothesized that incorporating this data into a machine-learning framework could improve multimer prediction accuracy beyond that of existing deep-learning methods. To this end, we developed SIDFold, a novel AlphaFold-based deep-learning network. SIDFold
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: Researchers developed SIDFold, a deep learning network that integrates Surface-Induced Dissociation Mass Spectrometry (SID-nMS) data with AlphaFold to improve the prediction of multimeric protein structures. By incorporating experimental connectivity and stoichiometry information from SID-nMS, SIDFold achieves higher accuracy than standard AlphaFold for protein complexes. This approach demonstrates how experimental mass spectrometry data can enhance computational structure prediction.

papers.title: Impact of QTL number, heritability and reference population size on the benefit of machine learning models over GBLUP in genomic prediction.
papers.abstract: With the successful application of machine learning (ML) in many areas, its potential for genomic prediction (GP) in animal and plant breeding has attracted growing attention. However, despite numerous studies, the benefit of ML models over GBLUP remains controversial. Some studies reported higher accuracy of ML than linear models with small reference populations, but this benefit often disappeared in larger datasets. Additionally, the number of QTL also affects the prediction accuracy of ML models. The aim of this study was to compare the performance of three ML models, random forest (RF), su
papers.summary: This study compares machine learning models (random forest, support vector machine, and gradient boosting) against the standard GBLUP method for genomic prediction in breeding, finding that ML models only outperform GBLUP when the number of quantitative trait loci (QTL) is small and the reference population is limited. The benefit of ML diminishes with larger reference populations or more QTL, indicating that GBLUP remains competitive for most practical scenarios.

papers.title: A vision-based MobileNet-GRU framework for continuous monitoring of student engagement and emotional states.
papers.abstract: Student engagement and emotional state monitoring remains a major challenge in modern education, particularly in online learning environments where direct instructor supervision is limited. Conventional evaluation approaches, such as surveys, quizzes, and feedback forms, rely heavily on subjective judgment and often fail to capture the dynamic temporal patterns of learner attention and behavioral engagement patterns. To address this limitation, this study proposes a vision-based framework that integrates efficient spatial feature extraction with temporal sequence modeling for continuous engage
papers.summary: This paper presents a vision-based deep learning framework that combines MobileNet for spatial feature extraction with GRU for temporal sequence modeling to continuously monitor student engagement and emotional states in online learning environments. The approach aims to overcome the limitations of subjective evaluation methods like surveys by capturing dynamic behavioral patterns from video data.

papers.title: CPP2Vec: A representation learning approach for cell-penetrating peptides prediction.
papers.abstract: Cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs) facilitate the delivery of a variety of therapeutic molecules across the plasma membrane, from small chemical substances to nucleic acid-based macromolecules, such as antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs). Among neutral ASOs, peptide nucleic acids (PNAs) and phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers (PMOs) have been extensively studied as potential medical treatments for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD), a severe genetic disease that causes muscle degeneration progressively. Over the last few decades, many in silico methods have emerged to detect novel CPPs, counter
papers.summary: CPP2Vec is a new machine learning method that uses representation learning to predict cell-penetrating peptides (CPPs), which are important for delivering therapeutic molecules like antisense oligonucleotides into cells. The approach aims to improve upon existing computational methods for identifying novel CPPs, which could aid in developing treatments for diseases such as Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy.

papers.title: Ensemble machine learning for predicting breast cancer recurrence and mortality using clinical and hemogram data.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of breast cancer recurrence remains difficult because prognosis varies significantly across molecular subtypes. This underscores the need for scalable, affordable, and interpretable prognostic tools. We developed machine learning models integrating hematological indices with clinicopathologic data to predict 2- and 10-year recurrence or all-case death. We retrospectively analyzed 4277 women with primary breast cancer (2008-2022). The cohort included hormone receptor-positive (HR + ), HER2-positive, and triple-negative (TNBC) subtypes. We trained multiple classifiers and int
papers.summary: This study developed machine learning models that combine blood test results with clinical data to predict breast cancer recurrence and death within 2 and 10 years. Using data from over 4,000 patients, the models performed well across different breast cancer subtypes, offering a potentially affordable and interpretable tool for prognosis.

papers.title: Uncovering the mechanism of DCLK3 in colorectal cancer progression through WGCNA and machine learning.
papers.abstract: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is a prevalent malignant tumor of the digestive tract with high morbidity and mortality rates. Although doublecortin like kinase 3 (DCLK3) is upregulated in CRC and promotes disease progression, its specific mechanisms remain unclear.
papers.summary: This study uses WGCNA and machine learning to investigate the role of DCLK3 in colorectal cancer progression, finding that DCLK3 is upregulated and promotes disease through specific mechanisms. The research provides insights into potential therapeutic targets for CRC.

papers.title: Enhancing tumor T cell antigen prediction by integrating deep protein representations.
papers.abstract: Cancer is a major threat to human health, and cancer immunotherapy is considered the most promising treatment option. It offers high effectiveness and precision, with fewer side effects than traditional treatments. Tumor T cell antigens (TTCA) are the proteins or protein fragments that stand on the surface of cancer cells and that are recognized by the immune system. However, physical experimental approaches to infer them might be costly and time-consuming, even though they are important for cancer immunotherapy.
papers.summary: This paper presents a computational method that improves the prediction of tumor T cell antigens by integrating deep protein representations. The approach aims to identify which protein fragments on cancer cells are recognized by the immune system, which is crucial for developing effective immunotherapies. By using deep learning on protein sequences, the method offers a faster and cheaper alternative to physical experiments.

papers.title: Serum Proteomic Profiling Implicates a Dysregulated Neurohormonal-Inflammatory Axis in Post-Fontan Sinus Tachycardia.
papers.abstract: Postoperative sinus tachycardia is a poorly understood complication following the Fontan procedure. The molecular signaling cascades triggering acute tachycardia remain uncharacterized, limiting therapeutic innovation. Here, we present a retrospective study leveraging serum proteomics and machine learning to identify the molecular drivers of postoperative Fontan sinus tachycardia.
papers.journal: Journal of the American Heart Association
papers.summary: This study used serum proteomics and machine learning to identify molecular drivers of sinus tachycardia after the Fontan procedure, a poorly understood complication. The findings implicate a dysregulated neurohormonal-inflammatory axis, offering potential therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Novel approach to early prediction of alzheimer's disease progression using integrated deep regulatory genetic neural network and optimized deep belief networks.
papers.abstract: To enhance the prediction progression of Alzheimer's Disease is very excavating process.
papers.summary: This study introduces a new computational method combining a deep regulatory genetic neural network with optimized deep belief networks to predict Alzheimer's disease progression earlier and more accurately. The approach integrates genetic and regulatory data to improve predictive performance over existing models.

papers.title: Investigating membrane protein conformational dynamics and noncovalent interactions via electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy.
papers.abstract: The advent of machine learning structural prediction tools has largely accelerated bottom-up structural biology and provides static Anfinsenian models with near-experimental accuracy, complementing X-ray crystallography and cryogenic electron microscopy. However, these techniques often overlook two fundamental aspects of biomolecular structure-function relationships: (i) conformational equilibria and (ii) transient noncovalent interactions. Indeed, these considerations are particularly important for integral membrane proteins (MPs), frequently serving mechanistic roles, such as conformational
papers.summary: This review discusses how machine learning structural prediction tools, while highly accurate for static models, often miss key aspects of membrane protein function: conformational dynamics and transient noncovalent interactions. The authors highlight the importance of electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy for capturing these dynamic features that are critical for understanding membrane protein mechanisms.

papers.title: InstaNovo-P: a de novo peptide sequencing model for phosphoproteomics.
papers.abstract: Phosphorylation, a crucial post-translational modification (PTM), plays a central role in cellular signaling and disease mechanisms. Mass spectrometry-based phosphoproteomics is widely used for system-wide characterization of phosphorylation events. However, traditional methods struggle with accurate phosphorylated site localization, complex search spaces, and detecting sequences outside the reference database. Advances in de novo peptide sequencing offer opportunities to address these limitations, but have yet to become integrated and adapted for phosphoproteomics datasets. Here, we present I
papers.summary: InstaNovo-P is a new deep learning model for de novo peptide sequencing specifically adapted for phosphoproteomics data. It addresses key challenges in identifying phosphorylation sites and sequences not found in reference databases, improving accuracy for this important post-translational modification.

papers.title: A unified vision-language model for precision oncology and biomarker prediction in neuroblastoma.
papers.abstract: Neuroblastoma is a leading cause of childhood cancer mortality, presenting persistent management challenges due to its biological heterogeneity and the limited accessibility of molecular profiling in routine practice. Here we present NEVA (NEuroblastoma Vision-language AI), a multimodal foundation model designed to address these barriers. Unlike conventional approaches that rely on frozen encoders and multiple instance learning, NEVA implements a pathologist-inspired hierarchical workflow with end-to-end optimization. Developed and evaluated in a large multi-institutional cohort of 1,238 patie
papers.summary: Researchers developed NEVA, a multimodal AI model that integrates pathology images and clinical data to predict biomarkers and outcomes in neuroblastoma, a childhood cancer. Tested on over 1,200 patients across multiple institutions, NEVA outperforms conventional methods by using a hierarchical, end-to-end learning approach inspired by pathologists. This work could make precision oncology more accessible by reducing reliance on costly molecular profiling.

papers.title: Decoding cancer circulating transcriptomic signatures with language models.
papers.abstract: Current liquid biopsy methods for multi-cancer detection using plasma cell-free RNA (cfRNA, short RNA fragments circulating in blood that can reflect disease states) typically rely on gene annotations, which can overlook signals from unannotated or repetitive genomic regions. We present GeneLLM, a Transformer-based model that directly processes the nucleotide sequences of human-mapped cfRNA reads to identify cancer-indicative signatures. By bypassing gene-level quantification, the model retains signals from transcriptomic dark matter. The model learns latent pseudo-biomarkers (prototype repres
papers.summary: GeneLLM is a Transformer-based language model that analyzes the nucleotide sequences of cell-free RNA directly, bypassing traditional gene annotation to detect cancer signatures from previously overlooked 'transcriptomic dark matter' in liquid biopsies. This approach improves multi-cancer detection by capturing signals from unannotated or repetitive genomic regions that standard methods miss.

papers.title: Mind the gap: An embedding guide to safely travel in sequence space.
papers.abstract: We present a hybrid approach combining a protein language model (pLM) with Monte Carlo (MC) sampling for generating enzyme mutants free of mutations deleterious for structural preservation. Given the amino acid sequence of the original enzyme and a set of residues for which the local environment should be conserved, i.e., the catalytic site, our approach generates mutants that differ vastly in the overall sequence while retaining the geometry of the conserved region, thereby representing promising candidates for further experimental screening. Unlike end-to-end deep-learning approaches, whose
papers.summary: This paper introduces a hybrid method that combines a protein language model with Monte Carlo sampling to generate enzyme mutants that preserve the structure of key regions like catalytic sites while allowing large changes elsewhere. The approach offers a way to explore diverse sequences without disrupting critical functions, making it useful for designing enzymes for experimental screening.

papers.title: LoMuS: Low-Rank Adaptation with Sequence Multi-representation Improves Protein Stability Prediction.
papers.abstract: Protein folding stability is a key determinant for understanding protein dynamics, including molecular function, pathogenicity, and protein engineering. Yet, accurate prediction of protein stability remains challenging due to high variability in available data, particularly when only sequence information is available and structural knowledge is limited or unavailable. In this work, we introduce LoMuS, a multi-representation-based deep learning model that predicts dataset-provided protein stability scores directly from the primary sequence. In the core of the model architecture, a fusion networ
papers.summary: LoMuS is a new deep learning model that predicts protein stability directly from amino acid sequences, even when structural information is unavailable. It uses a multi-representation approach with a fusion network to handle the high variability in stability data, improving prediction accuracy.

papers.title: RLAnOxPeptide: An Integrated Framework Combining Transformer and reinforcement learning for Efficient Antioxidant Peptide Prediction and Innovative Design.
papers.abstract: Bioactive peptides exhibit immense potential in pharmaceutical and food science domains, with antioxidant peptides (AOPs) garnering significant attention for their roles in scavenging free radicals. However, traditional discovery methods are inefficient and costly. This study introduces RLAnOxPeptide, an integrated computational framework that merges machine learning and reinforcement learning for the efficient prediction and de novo design of AOPs.
papers.summary: RLAnOxPeptide is a new computational framework that combines transformer-based machine learning with reinforcement learning to predict and design antioxidant peptides more efficiently than traditional lab methods. This approach could accelerate the discovery of these peptides, which are important for fighting oxidative stress in pharmaceutical and food science applications.

papers.title: Machine learning reveals mechanisms and feedstock effects on potassium and magnesium uptake by wheat in biochar-amended soil in greenhouse.
papers.abstract: Despite the potential of biochar to enhance crop nutrient uptake, the complex, feedstock-dependent interactions among biochar properties, soil biological processes, and plant root traits remain poorly understood. Hence, this study employed machine learning algorithms (Linear regression and Random Forest) with advanced feature selection methods (Correlation-based Feature Subset Selection Evaluator, Correlation-Based Attribute Evaluator, Principal Components Attribute Transformer, and Stepwise Regression) to predict potassium (K+) and magnesium (Mg2+) uptake by wheat (Triticum aestivum L.) roots
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict how different biochar feedstocks affect potassium and magnesium uptake in wheat roots, revealing that feedstock type significantly influences nutrient availability through complex interactions with soil biology and root traits. The findings provide a data-driven framework for optimizing biochar applications to improve crop nutrient uptake.

papers.title: GenART: Reading the genome's language with adaptive "words".
papers.abstract: Tokenization is both a prerequisite and a central challenge for genomic language models, due to the inherent difficulty of delineating meaningful segments within continuous DNA sequences. We present GenART, a genomic language model framework that dynamically segments DNA into variable-length words directly from raw sequences without manual annotation. Its key adaptive tokenization module infers biologically meaningful boundaries from multi-scale contextual signals during pretraining. GenART demonstrates competitive performance across diverse tasks, outperforming leading approaches. Word bounda
papers.summary: GenART is a new genomic language model that learns to automatically segment DNA sequences into variable-length 'words' without needing manual annotation. By dynamically identifying biologically meaningful boundaries during training, it outperforms existing methods on a range of genomic tasks. This adaptive tokenization approach addresses a key challenge in applying language models to DNA.

papers.title: Prediction of in-hospital atrial fibrillation after acute myocardial infarction: a cross-cohort study in Italian and Finnish patients.
papers.abstract: The development of atrial fibrillation (AF) during hospitalization for acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is common and associated with worse prognosis. We aimed to train and, for the first time, cross-nationally validate a machine learning model to predict AF developing during the cardiac intensive care unit (CICU) stay in post-AMI patients admitted in sinus rhythm, using only routine, non-electrocardiographic clinical parameters available at CICU admission.
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model to predict which patients hospitalized for acute myocardial infarction will develop atrial fibrillation during their intensive care stay, using only routine clinical data available at admission. The model was trained on Italian patients and validated on Finnish patients, demonstrating cross-national generalizability. This could help identify high-risk patients early for closer monitoring and preventive interventions.

papers.title: A multi-view feature fusion framework with interpretable graph convolution for predicting microbe-drug associations.
papers.abstract: Predicting associations between human microbes and drugs (MDA) is a critical step in drug development and precision medicine. Although various computational approaches have been proposed, many existing models still struggle to reveal the key features and interaction mechanisms that drive their predictions, and their decision processes remain difficult to interpret and validate biologically. To address these limitations, we propose IDEAL (Interpretability-Driven Evolvable Attentive Learning for Microbe-Drug Association), a multi-view framework that integrates drug network topological attributes
papers.summary: This paper presents IDEAL, a new computational framework that uses graph convolution and multi-view feature fusion to predict associations between microbes and drugs. The key advance is that the model is designed to be interpretable, helping researchers understand which features drive the predictions for better biological validation.

papers.title: AI-guided CRISPR screening reveals therapeutic targets in psoriasis.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis affects over 125 million people globally. Biologics targeting the IL-17/IL-17RA axis are effective but require systemic administration, are costly, and are unsuitable for some patients. Developing topical small-molecule alternatives requires a better understanding of how IL-17 receptor A (IL17RA) is regulated in keratinocytes, the principal effector cells of psoriatic lesions. Here, we report a genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen for regulators of surface IL17RA in primary human epidermal keratinocytes. We prioritize hits using experimental enrichment together with VirtualCRISPR, a la
papers.summary: This study uses a genome-wide CRISPR knockout screen combined with a machine learning tool called VirtualCRISPR to identify regulators of IL-17 receptor A in keratinocytes, uncovering potential therapeutic targets for psoriasis. The approach prioritizes hits that could lead to topical small-molecule treatments, offering an alternative to systemic biologics for the over 125 million people affected by psoriasis.

papers.title: CryoROLE: describing large inter-domain rotation in single particle cryo-EM.
papers.abstract: In single particle cryo-EM, analysis of continuous conformational heterogeneity has always been challenging. Both linear and deep learning-based methods treat conformational heterogeneity as perturbations to the consensus average conformation, limiting their capability in analyzing large protein motions. While classic conformational classifications are capable of handling large domain motion, they bin continuous protein dynamics into discrete static substates. Here, we present cryoROLE, a computational tool that extracts the continuous conformational dynamics embedded in the static composite m
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: CryoROLE is a new computational tool that extracts continuous conformational dynamics from single particle cryo-EM data, specifically designed to handle large inter-domain rotations that previous methods struggle with. It overcomes limitations of both deep learning approaches (which treat motions as small perturbations) and classic classification methods (which discretize continuous dynamics).

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs), particularly transformer-based architectures, are being applied across bioinformatics domains including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. The authors discuss the components and training paradigms of LLMs and highlight their potential to address complex biological challenges beyond traditional natural language processing tasks.

papers.title: Accurate, comprehensive gene annotation and ortholog identification across thousands of vertebrate genomes with TOGA2.
papers.abstract: Inferring orthologs and annotating coding genes remain central challenges in genomics, evident by the growing gap between assembled and annotated genomes. TOGA (Tool to infer Orthologs from Genome Alignments) addresses this challenge by integrating gene annotation and orthology inference. Here, we present TOGA2, the next generation of TOGA, which substantially improves annotation completeness, accuracy, scalability, and orthology inference. TOGA2 leverages exon-level orthology and introduces an exon-wise annotation procedure that reduces memory usage 513-fold and runtime 6.1-fold. We show that
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: TOGA2 is an improved computational tool that simultaneously annotates genes and identifies their evolutionary counterparts (orthologs) across thousands of vertebrate genomes. It achieves over 500-fold reduction in memory usage and 6-fold faster runtime compared to its predecessor, helping close the gap between genome assembly and annotation.

papers.title: RD-OMICS: An Integrative Multi-Omics Data Inventory in Rare Diseases.
papers.abstract: Rare diseases (RD) impact over 30 million individuals in the United States, yet fewer than 5% of the identified conditions have FDA-approved treatments. Progress in RD research is hindered by small patient cohorts, biological heterogeneity, and the fragmented, inconsistently annotated publicly available omics data, which limits integrative analysis and translational discovery. Here, we present RD-OMICS, a data inventory with integrated and structured RD omics data from Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO), in the form of a knowledge graph. We developed a metadata harmonization pipeline that combines
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: RD-OMICS is a new data inventory that integrates and structures rare disease omics data from public repositories into a knowledge graph, addressing the fragmentation and inconsistent annotation that hinders research. By harmonizing metadata, it enables more effective integrative analysis and translational discovery for rare diseases, which currently lack treatments for over 95% of identified conditions.

papers.title: Accurate prediction of asparagine deamidation in biologics using advanced machine learning models.
papers.abstract: The spontaneous deamidation of asparagine residues remains a major obstacle to the stability and efficacy of protein therapeutics. Currently available models in the literature for predicting deamidation liabilities can suffer from limited generalizability, likely due to biases such as sequence similarity within datasets. In this study, we built machine learning models using protein language models (PLMs, e.g. ESM2) and graph neural networks (GNNs), trained on a comprehensive dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 protein molecules. To address the critical issue of data leakage, we imple
papers.summary: This study developed machine learning models using protein language models and graph neural networks to predict asparagine deamidation in protein therapeutics, trained on a comprehensive dataset of 591 asparagine sites from over 105 protein molecules. The models address data leakage issues and achieve high accuracy, offering a robust tool for improving the stability of biologic drugs.

papers.title: scGenoByte: a GenoByte embedding transformer with biological priors for cell type annotation.
papers.abstract: Effective cell representation learning is crucial for accurate cell annotation and the deciphering of cellular heterogeneity in single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) analysis. Current foundation models have achieved superior performance compared with traditional methods. However, due to data sparsity and the complexity of model, existing methods often compromise by selecting highly variable genes or filtering for nonzero expressions, which discard potentially significant genes. Thus, modeling the complete transcriptome for cell representation remains computationally challenging; we present sc
papers.summary: scGenoByte is a new transformer-based model that uses biological priors to embed the full transcriptome for cell type annotation from single-cell RNA-seq data, overcoming limitations of previous methods that discard potentially important genes due to data sparsity. By modeling the complete transcriptome, it improves cell representation learning and annotation accuracy.

papers.title: Affinage: genome-scale mechanistic gene annotation from the published literature.
papers.abstract: Understanding the mechanistic function of a gene is a critical starting point for biology. However, for much of the human proteome that knowledge is scattered across thousands of primary papers or remains poorly established, while the curated databases biologists rely on can lag years behind recent literature. Large language models can now read and synthesize that literature on demand, but doing so faithfully for many genes is an expensive, non-reproducible retrieval session that does not scale across users. Here, we present Affinage, an LLM pipeline that performs this retrieval and mechanisti
papers.summary: Affinage is a new LLM pipeline that automatically reads and synthesizes the published literature to produce mechanistic gene annotations at genome scale, addressing the lag in curated databases. It aims to provide faithful, reproducible, and scalable retrieval of gene function knowledge from thousands of papers.

papers.title: Molecular Adaptations of Thermophilic Proteins-From Mechanistic Understanding to Machine Learning Approaches.
papers.abstract: Thermophilic proteins are known for their stability and activity at elevated temperatures and hence serve as useful models for engineering enzymes with enhanced stability. The stability of these proteins is governed by both thermodynamic and kinetic factors that affect the folding-unfolding equilibrium and the rate of irreversible denaturation. Structural studies have shown that enhanced thermostability often results from multiple cooperative interactions, including improved hydrophobic packing, strengthened electrostatic interactions, and optimized hydrogen-bonding networks. Comparative studi
papers.summary: This review discusses how thermophilic proteins maintain stability at high temperatures through cooperative structural adaptations like improved hydrophobic packing and electrostatic interactions, and highlights how machine learning approaches are now being used to understand and engineer these properties.

papers.title: A multi-chip integrated analysis employing machine learning techniques identifies biomarkers for diagnosing PCOS and its comorbidity with T2D.
papers.abstract: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common endocrine disorder closely linked to type 2 diabetes (T2D). Although linked, potential biomarkers for PCOS have not yet been sufficiently studied. This study aimed to identify diagnostic biomarkers linked to PCOS through an integrative analysis of multi-chip data and to examine their relationship with T2D. We used multi-chip datasets from public databases, conducted differential expression analysis to screen genes, and performed cross-disease gene overlap analysis using Venn diagrams. We conducted functional enrichment analyses, including gene ontol
papers.summary: This study used machine learning to analyze multiple gene expression datasets and identify diagnostic biomarkers for polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) and its comorbidity with type 2 diabetes (T2D). The authors identified overlapping genes between the two conditions and performed functional enrichment analyses to characterize their roles.

papers.title: Non-invasive diagnostic model for myocarditis using cardiac magnetic resonance radiomics.
papers.abstract: Myocarditis is an inflammatory myocardial disease with high clinical heterogeneity, and its early diagnosis relies on invasive endomyocardial biopsy (EMB) or advanced cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) techniques with poor accessibility in primary hospitals. Conventional CMR interpretation is subjective with inter-observer variability, and single-modality radiomics models have insufficient diagnostic accuracy. This study aimed to construct and validate a machine learning model integrating multimodal CMR radiomics features and clinical parameters for early non-invasive and accurate diagnosis of m
papers.journal: Quantitative imaging in medicine and surgery
papers.summary: This study developed a machine learning model that combines multimodal cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) radiomics features with clinical parameters to improve non-invasive diagnosis of myocarditis. The model aims to overcome limitations of subjective CMR interpretation and low accuracy of single-modality approaches, potentially reducing reliance on invasive biopsies.

papers.title: Spatial-temporal and physical constrained deep learning model for simultaneous T1 and T2 reconstruction and mapping (STEP).
papers.abstract: Magnetic resonance (MR) parametric maps provide quantitative tissue characteristics that are valuable for medical diagnosis. However, existing T1 and T2 measurement techniques confront challenges such as prolonged reconstruction/fitting times and the requirement for multi-sequence image registration, limiting the clinical applicability of quantitative parameter mapping. The development of compressed sensing combined with parallel imaging technology has improved reconstruction efficiency. However, traditional two-step workflow lacks spatial constraints for parameter mapping and suffers from slo
papers.journal: Quantitative imaging in medicine and surgery
papers.summary: This paper presents a deep learning model (STEP) that simultaneously reconstructs T1 and T2 parametric maps from undersampled MRI data, addressing the limitations of slow reconstruction and the need for image registration. The model integrates spatial-temporal and physical constraints to improve efficiency and accuracy, potentially enhancing clinical applicability of quantitative MRI.

papers.title: Learning millisecond protein dynamics from what is missing in NMR spectra.
papers.abstract: Many proteins' biological functions rely on interconversions between multiple conformations occurring at micro-to millisecond (µs-ms) timescales. A lack of standardized, large-scale experimental data has hindered obtaining a more predictive understanding of these motions. After curating >100 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) relaxation datasets, we realized an observable for µs-ms dynamics might be hiding in plain sight. Millisecond dynamics can cause NMR signals to broaden beyond detection, leaving some residues not assigned in the chemical shift datasets of ~10,000 proteins deposited in the B
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: The researchers discovered that missing NMR signals in chemical shift datasets can serve as a proxy for detecting millisecond-timescale protein dynamics, which are crucial for biological function. By curating over 100 NMR relaxation datasets and analyzing ~10,000 deposited protein datasets, they found that unassigned residues often indicate motions occurring at micro-to-millisecond timescales, providing a large-scale resource for studying these important conformational changes.

papers.title: Collective fluctuations underlying nanobody inhibitory activity targeting B. anthracis S-layers revealed by multiscale simulations.
papers.abstract: Nanobodies (Nbs) that depolymerize bacterial surface-layers (S-layers) offer a route to antivirulence therapeutics, but their mechanisms have been difficult to infer from binding structures alone. In B. anthracis, several Nbs bind to Sap, the S-layer protein that assembles into a paracrystalline lattice surrounding the cell. Despite similar binding poses and sequences, only a subset of these ("inhibitory") Nbs induce disassembly of the protein lattice, ultimately abrogating pathogenicity. In this study, we leveraged multiscale simulations to test whether representative Nb-induced fluctuations
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study uses multiscale simulations to investigate why only some nanobodies (Nbs) that bind to the B. anthracis S-layer protein Sap are able to disassemble the protective protein lattice and inhibit pathogenicity. The simulations reveal that inhibitory Nbs induce specific collective fluctuations in the Sap lattice that are absent with non-inhibitory Nbs, providing a mechanistic explanation beyond static binding structures. This work offers insights for designing antivirulence therapeutics targeting bacterial S-layers.

papers.title: MiRformer: a dual-transformer-encoder framework for predicting microRNA-mRNA interactions from paired sequences.
papers.abstract: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression by binding to target messenger RNAs (mRNAs), inducing translational repression or mRNA degradation. Accurate prediction of miRNA-mRNA interactions and precise localization of binding and cleavage sites are critical for understanding post-transcriptional regulation and for enabling RNA therapeutics. Existing computational methods often rely on handcrafted or indirect features, scale poorly to kilobase-long mRNA sequences, or provide limited interpretability.
papers.summary: MiRformer is a new deep learning model that uses a dual-transformer-encoder architecture to predict which microRNAs bind to which messenger RNAs directly from their sequences, without needing handcrafted features. It can handle long mRNA sequences and also identifies the precise binding and cleavage sites, improving interpretability over existing methods. This could advance understanding of gene regulation and aid in designing RNA-based therapies.

papers.title: A novel transformer model of protein domains for viral taxonomy classification.
papers.abstract: Viruses with carefully curated taxonomic assignments (such as those in the ICTV taxonomy) still represent only a small fraction of viruses identified through sequencing data from virome or microbiome projects. It is therefore critical to develop methods that can assign viruses at multiple taxonomic ranks, so that a virus deemed novel at a given rank may still be placed into a higher-level taxon. Sequence-similarity-based approaches can classify viruses that share substantial genomic similarity with known viruses (e.g. those belonging to the same species or genus); however, their performance dr
papers.summary: This paper presents a novel transformer model that uses protein domain information to classify viruses into taxonomic ranks, even when they share little sequence similarity with known viruses. The approach addresses a key challenge in viromics: assigning taxonomy to the vast majority of viruses that cannot be classified by traditional sequence-similarity methods.

papers.title: NPBIP: predicting binding preferences of uncharacterized nucleic-acid-binding proteins.
papers.abstract: Nucleic-acid-binding proteins (NBPs) are crucial regulators of gene expression, recognizing specific RNA or DNA binding sites. While high-throughput experiments have generated vast amounts of binding data, a significant challenge remains in predicting binding affinities of a novel query NBP to any nucleic-acid sequence. Current computational methods often require prior experimental data for the query NBP or are limited to predictions over predefined short RNA or DNA sequences.
papers.summary: NPBIP is a new computational tool that predicts how any nucleic-acid-binding protein (NBP) will bind to RNA or DNA sequences, even if no prior experimental data exists for that protein. It overcomes limitations of existing methods that require pre-existing binding data or can only handle short, predefined sequences. This makes it useful for studying uncharacterized NBPs involved in gene regulation.

papers.title: PU-GRAIL: residue-level graph learning for identifying protective bacterial antigens under positive-unlabeled supervision.
papers.abstract: The identification of protective antigens is fundamentally constrained by sparse annotations and pervasive label uncertainty in reverse vaccinology. Protective antigen-antibody interactions are mediated by a limited subset of surface-accessible residues that form spatially coherent epitopes. This motivates modeling antigenicity at the residue level, where 3D structure provides critical context for functional immune recognition. Moreover, antigen datasets are inherently positive unlabeled: unannotated proteins may contain hidden positives, making reliable negatives difficult to obtain.
papers.summary: PU-GRAIL is a new computational method that predicts protective bacterial antigens by analyzing protein structures at the residue level, using a graph neural network to identify surface-accessible epitopes. It addresses the challenge of sparse and uncertain labels in vaccine target discovery by employing a positive-unlabeled learning framework that does not require reliable negative examples. This approach improves the identification of vaccine candidates by leveraging 3D structural context and handling hidden positives in unannotated data.

papers.title: eGoT: enhanced graph-of-thoughts for multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation in biomedicine.
papers.abstract: Research on biological mechanisms and disease processes is limited by fragmented findings across unstructured text in publications. Question answering and hypothesis generation that can reason across multiple sources can overcome this limitation. However, Large language models (LLMs) are prone to inaccuracies and lack clear provenance to primary evidence. Retrieval augmented generation approaches that have provenance to the original source of evidence address these shortcomings. However, the response richness is dependent on the retrieval process design. Current approaches often fail to produc
papers.summary: This paper introduces eGoT, a method that enhances graph-of-thoughts reasoning to improve multi-hop knowledge retrieval and hypothesis generation from biomedical literature. By combining structured reasoning with retrieval-augmented generation, eGoT aims to produce more accurate and well-sourced answers compared to standard LLM approaches.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: This paper introduces Agentomics, an AI agent system that autonomously designs and implements high-performing machine learning solutions for biomedical problems, outperforming both existing automated ML tools and human-engineered models. The system leverages large language models to flexibly generate reproducible codebases tailored to evolving biomedical data.

papers.title: Predicting antibody self-association with sequence-structure fusion models: the central role of CSI-BLI in early developability screening.
papers.abstract: Antibody-based biologics are expanding rapidly, yet challenges in development from self-association, high viscosity, aggregation, and unfavorable clearance underscore the need for accurate in silico screening. Clone self-interaction biolayer interferometry (CSI-BLI) is a plate-based, low-material assay of weak, reversible self-association that serves as an early proxy for high-concentration viscosity and a complementary predictor of in vivo clearance. In a panel of 246 monoclonal antibodies, CSI-BLI moderately correlates with viscosity; further, in hFcRn Tg32 mice (41 antibodies), CSI-BLI stro
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that CSI-BLI, a plate-based assay measuring weak antibody self-association, serves as an effective early proxy for high-concentration viscosity and in vivo clearance. Using a panel of 246 monoclonal antibodies, the authors show that combining sequence and structure features with machine learning can predict CSI-BLI outcomes, enabling early developability screening.

papers.title: Linguistics and human brain: a perspective of computational neuroscience.
papers.abstract: Elucidating the language-brain relationship requires bridging the methodological gap between linguistics' abstract theoretical frameworks and neuroscience's empirical neural data. As an interdisciplinary cornerstone, computational neuroscience formalizes language's hierarchical and dynamic structures into testable neural representation models through modeling, simulation, and data analysis, enabling computational dialogue between linguistic hypotheses and neural mechanisms. Recent advances in deep learning, particularly large language models (LLMs), have further advanced this inquiry: their hi
papers.summary: This perspective paper discusses how computational neuroscience bridges the gap between linguistic theory and neural data, highlighting how deep learning and large language models (LLMs) enable testable models of language processing in the brain. The authors argue that these computational approaches formalize language's hierarchical structures into neural representation models, advancing our understanding of the language-brain relationship.

papers.title: Growth factor thermostability dataset derived from experimental and computational approaches to accelerate cultured meat development.
papers.abstract: This project compiled experimentally measured and computationally predicted thermostability data for growth factors (GFs) commonly used in cultured meat and seafood (CM) production. Data were generated on recombinant GFs using thermal shift assays and combined with publicly available data using a structured literature curation workflow developed using a Large Language Model (LLM) with manual validation. In addition, in silico protein stability calculations were generated using FoldX. Data such as GF identity, species origin, recombinant host, variant information, method of measurement, and exp
papers.summary: This paper presents a dataset of thermostability measurements for growth factors used in cultured meat production, combining experimental assays, literature mining with an LLM, and computational predictions from FoldX. The resource aims to accelerate the development of more stable, cost-effective growth factors for cultivated meat and seafood.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses a global self-attentive pooling mechanism to better capture important features from both drug and protein structures, potentially reducing the need for costly biological experiments in drug discovery.

papers.title: An interpretable AutoML-based prediction model for enteral nutrition intolerance in severe pulmonary tuberculosis patients and development of a clinical decision system.
papers.abstract: This study aims to construct an AutoML-based predictive model for enteral nutrition intolerance (ENI) in severe pulmonary tuberculosis (PTB) patients and develop a visualized clinical decision support system to inform personalized nutrition management.
papers.summary: This study developed an automated machine learning (AutoML) model to predict enteral nutrition intolerance in severe pulmonary tuberculosis patients, and created a visual clinical decision support system to guide personalized nutrition management. The model helps clinicians identify high-risk patients early and tailor nutritional interventions accordingly.

papers.title: Deep learning for cardiovascular disease: a comprehensive review of detection and risk forecasting.
papers.abstract: The biggest health threat to the global population is cardiovascular disease (CVD), which afflicts almost one-third of the global population and causes considerable monetary and social losses. Risk analysis should be performed in a timely and appropriate manner to enhance clinical practice and preventive interventions. The emergence of advanced data modalities, such as wearable sensors, medical imaging, electronic health records (EHRs), and genomic platforms, has led to a paradigm shift in the holistic assessment of CVD risk through multimodal data integration. This systematic review is a meth
papers.summary: This review examines how deep learning methods can improve detection and risk prediction for cardiovascular disease by integrating diverse data sources like wearable sensors, medical imaging, and genomic data. The authors highlight that multimodal data fusion represents a paradigm shift for comprehensive CVD risk assessment, potentially enhancing clinical practice and preventive interventions.

papers.title: Bioinformatics analysis reveals the association of bile acid metabolism-related genes with sepsis.
papers.abstract: Sepsis, is a life-threatening syndrome triggered by infection. Bile acid metabolism may be involved in the pathogenesis of sepsis, the underlying association has not yet been elucidated. Thus, we aimed to screen for bile acid metabolism-related biomarkers of sepsis and discover the potential association.
papers.summary: This study uses bioinformatics to identify bile acid metabolism-related genes that may serve as biomarkers for sepsis, suggesting a link between bile acid metabolism and the disease's pathogenesis. The findings could help in understanding sepsis better and potentially lead to new diagnostic or therapeutic approaches.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new computational method that combines a Graph Transformer with CNN-BiLSTM and cross-attention to better predict which drugs interact with which proteins. By jointly capturing the 3D structure of drug molecules and the sequence context of proteins, it outperforms existing deep learning approaches on benchmark datasets. This could accelerate drug discovery and repurposing by reducing the need for costly experiments.

papers.title: PlantFormer: a precise plant disease segmentation network with interactive backbone and global-anisotropic context aggregation.
papers.abstract: Precise plant disease segmentation in real-world agricultural environments presents challenges that general-purpose models often fail to address, primarily due to the anisotropic spread of lesions, blurred biological boundaries, and severe background dominance. To overcome these bottlenecks, this paper proposes PlantFormer, an end-to-end network that effectively integrates and adapts advanced architectural components to address these domain-specific issues. Specifically, PlantFormer employs an InteractSwin Backbone with a Cross-Level Fusion (CLF) module to preserve early pathological details.
papers.summary: PlantFormer is a new deep learning network designed to precisely segment plant diseases in real-world agricultural images, overcoming challenges like irregular lesion shapes and blurred boundaries. It uses an interactive backbone and a cross-level fusion module to preserve fine pathological details, outperforming general-purpose models. This work provides a specialized tool for automated plant disease monitoring.
